---
title: "4.3 Array Creation and Access"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Arrays store many values of one type

An <span class="term">array</span> (数组) is a fixed-size object that stores multiple values of the same type.

![Row lockers analogy](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-3-array-creation-and-access/rowLockers.jpg){fig-align="center" width="42%"}

Each element is accessed by an index.

## Declaring and Creating Arrays

### Type, brackets, and size

```java
int[] scores = new int[5];
String[] names = new String[3];
```

Rules:

::: {.tight-list}
- the array length is fixed after creation
- indices start at `0`
- last valid index is `length - 1`
- numeric arrays created with `new` start with default values such as `0`
:::

## Quick Check

### Create an array

Which line creates an array that can hold 10 `double` values?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `double scores = new double[10];`
- B. `double[] scores = new double[10];`
- C. `int[] scores = new double[10];`
- D. `double[] scores = new double;`
:::

Answer: <span class="mark">B</span>.

## Array Indices

### Index 0 is the first element

![Array indices](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-3-array-creation-and-access/arrayIndicies.png){fig-align="center" width="56%"}

```java
int[] values = {4, 7, 9};
System.out.println(values[0]);
System.out.println(values[2]);
```

Output:

```text
4
9
```

## Initializer Lists

### Create and fill at the same time

![Integer and String arrays](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-3-array-creation-and-access/intAndStringArrays.png){fig-align="center" width="48%"}

```java
int[] nums = {1, 2, 3, 4};
String[] words = {"cat", "dog", "bird"};
```

The length is determined by the number of initializer values.

## Array Length

### Use `.length`, not a method call

```java
String[] words = {"cat", "dog", "bird"};
System.out.println(words.length);
```

Output:

```text
3
```

Arrays use the field `.length`; strings use the method `.length()`.

## Access and Modify Values

### Use an index on the left side to update

```java
int[] scores = {80, 90, 100};
scores[1] = 95;
System.out.println(scores[1]);
```

Output:

```text
95
```

An index outside `0` through `length - 1` causes an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.

## Groupwork Coding Challenge

### Countries Array

Starter:

```java
String[] countries = {"Brazil", "China", "France", "Kenya"};

System.out.println(countries[0]);
System.out.println(countries[countries.length - 1]);
countries[1] = "Canada";
```

Add two more countries, print the length, and modify one element.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- declare and create arrays with the correct type and size
- explain that array indices start at `0`
- use `array.length` to find the number of elements
- access and modify values with bracket notation
- identify the last valid index as `array.length - 1`
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
